source_paper_title: Splitting Guarantees for Prophet Inequalities via Nonlinear Systems

Section 6 (Final Remarks), page 28 (arXiv v2, 17 Apr 2025).

Finding an analytical formula for the approximation ratio of \((k, n)\)-SPI remains a problem, but our findings offer a potential avenue toward this goal.
title: Determine the exact approximation ratio for i.i.d. k-selection prophet inequalities
background_latex:
Consider the i.i.d. 
\(k\)-selection prophet inequality (also called \((k,n)\)-SPI). Let \(n\ge k\) and let \(X_1,\dots,X_n\) be i.i.d. nonnegative random variables drawn from a known continuous distribution \(F\) supported on \(\mathbb{R}_+\).

A sequential policy \(\pi\) observes \(X_t\) one-by-one and must decide irrevocably whether to accept \(X_t\), subject to accepting at most \(k\) items in total. The policy’s expected reward is \(\mathbb{E}[\sum_{t\in S_\pi} X_t]\), where \(S_\pi\subseteq\{1,\dots,n\}\) is the random set of accepted indices with \(|S_\pi|\le k\).

The offline (prophet) benchmark is the expected sum of the \(k\) largest values among \(X_1,\dots,X_n\), i.e.
\[\mathrm{OPT}_{n,k}(F)=\sum_{j=n-k+1}^n \mathbb{E}[X_{(j)}],\]
where \(X_{(1)}\le\cdots\le X_{(n)}\) denote the order statistics.

For a fixed \((n,k)\), define \(\gamma_{n,k}\) as the worst-case optimal approximation ratio over distributions:
\[\gamma_{n,k}=\inf_F \ \sup_{\pi} \ \frac{\mathbb{E}[\sum_{t\in S_\pi} X_t]}{\mathrm{OPT}_{n,k}(F)}.\]
Equivalently, \(\gamma_{n,k}\) is the best constant such that for every \(F\) there exists a policy achieving expected reward at least \(\gamma_{n,k}\,\mathrm{OPT}_{n,k}(F)\).

One may also consider the worst-case over horizon length, \(\inf_{n\ge k}\gamma_{n,k}\), and/or asymptotic regimes such as \(n\to\infty\) for fixed \(k\). For \(k=1\), the tight constant is known (approximately \(0.745\)) via the Hill--Kertz differential equation and matching algorithms; for \(k\ge 2\), the corresponding exact constant is not known.
problem_definition_latex: Determine (in closed/analytical form) the optimal worst-case approximation ratio \(\gamma_{n,k}\) for the i.i.d. \((k,n)\)-selection prophet inequality, for \(k\ge 2\). In particular, determine an analytical formula for the (worst-case) approximation ratio in this setting (e.g., as a function of \(n\) and \(k\), or for the worst-case value \(\inf_{n\ge k}\gamma_{n,k}\), or in the asymptotic regime as \(n\to\infty\) with fixed \(k\)).
latex:
Consider the i.i.d. 
\(k\)-selection prophet inequality (also called \((k,n)\)-SPI). Let \(n\ge k\) and let \(X_1,\dots,X_n\) be i.i.d. nonnegative random variables drawn from a known continuous distribution \(F\) supported on \(\mathbb{R}_+\).

A sequential policy \(\pi\) observes \(X_t\) one-by-one and must decide irrevocably whether to accept \(X_t\), subject to accepting at most \(k\) items in total. The policy’s expected reward is \(\mathbb{E}[\sum_{t\in S_\pi} X_t]\), where \(S_\pi\subseteq\{1,\dots,n\}\) is the random set of accepted indices with \(|S_\pi|\le k\).

The offline (prophet) benchmark is the expected sum of the \(k\) largest values among \(X_1,\dots,X_n\), i.e.
\[\mathrm{OPT}_{n,k}(F)=\sum_{j=n-k+1}^n \mathbb{E}[X_{(j)}],\]
where \(X_{(1)}\le\cdots\le X_{(n)}\) denote the order statistics.

For a fixed \((n,k)\), define \(\gamma_{n,k}\) as the worst-case optimal approximation ratio over distributions:
\[\gamma_{n,k}=\inf_F \ \sup_{\pi} \ \frac{\mathbb{E}[\sum_{t\in S_\pi} X_t]}{\mathrm{OPT}_{n,k}(F)}.\]
Equivalently, \(\gamma_{n,k}\) is the best constant such that for every \(F\) there exists a policy achieving expected reward at least \(\gamma_{n,k}\,\mathrm{OPT}_{n,k}(F)\).

One may also consider the worst-case over horizon length, \(\inf_{n\ge k}\gamma_{n,k}\), and/or asymptotic regimes such as \(n\to\infty\) for fixed \(k\). For \(k=1\), the tight constant is known (approximately \(0.745\)) via the Hill--Kertz differential equation and matching algorithms; for \(k\ge 2\), the corresponding exact constant is not known.

Determine (in closed/analytical form) the optimal worst-case approximation ratio \(\gamma_{n,k}\) for the i.i.d. \((k,n)\)-selection prophet inequality, for \(k\ge 2\). In particular, determine an analytical formula for the (worst-case) approximation ratio in this setting (e.g., as a function of \(n\) and \(k\), or for the worst-case value \(\inf_{n\ge k}\gamma_{n,k}\), or in the asymptotic regime as \(n\to\infty\) with fixed \(k\)).
open_problem_quote: Finding an analytical formula for the approximation ratio of \((k, n)\)-SPI remains a problem, but our findings offer a potential avenue toward this goal.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for an analytical characterization of an optimal worst-case ratio over all continuous distributions and optimal policies, not a specific conjectured bound with a finite, checkable witness. Automated search could provide numerical estimates for fixed grids or parametric families, but it is unlikely to yield definitive counterexamples to an unspecified analytical formula.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can programmatically evaluate candidate policies and distributions numerically for fixed \((n,k)\) via discretized LP/DP formulations to generate evidence toward formulas, giving an automatic fitness signal. However, turning this into a proof-level analytical formula (and validating it over all distributions and all \(n\)) is not directly automatable, so suitability is limited.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
